aliases: the Besht (הבעש"ט, acronym of Ba'al Shem Tov)
the Baal Shem Tov ('Master of the Good Name')

roles: founder of Hasidism (in the movement's account)
summary: Israel ben Eliezer (c. 1698-1760), known as the Baal Shem Tov, 'Master of the Good Name' (acronym the Besht), was a mystic and popular healer in Podolia (in the southeastern Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, now Ukraine) whom the Hasidic movement reveres as its founder. He taught the divine presence in all things, joyful and wholehearted devotion, and prayer offered with mystical intention and cleaving to God (devekut). He committed almost nothing to writing; his teaching is known through the traditions of his disciples, the hagiographic Shivchei ha-Besht, and his letter to his brother-in-law Gershon of Kutov, the 'Holy Epistle' (Iggeret ha-Kodesh).
description:
Israel ben Eliezer, called the Baal Shem Tov, 'Master of the Good Name' (in the Jewish tradition, one who could invoke the divine names for healing and protection; the title is commonly abbreviated to the acronym the Besht), lived c. 1698-1760 in Podolia and the surrounding regions of the southeastern Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, in lands now largely in Ukraine. In the standard scholarship he was a ba'al shem, a popular healer and mystic who used amulets, herbal remedies, and the mystical power of the divine names, and who gathered around himself a circle of associates and disciples. The Hasidic movement reveres him as its founder, and much of what is 'known' of his life comes in the form of hagiography; the modern historical study of him has therefore turned on the effort to distinguish the recoverable historical figure from the later legend.

The teaching associated with the Besht, transmitted through his disciples and their writings, centres on a few connected themes, given here as his and the movement's attributed teaching rather than as claims the editor affirms. God's presence is held to fill and animate all things, so that the worshipper is to keep an awareness of God constant, 'before me always'. Devotion is to be wholehearted and marked above all by joy (simcha): the disciples' collections record him teaching that one should serve God 'with simplicity' and 'with joy', resisting the sadness and melancholy that obstruct divine service. Prayer is to be offered with kavvanah, mystical intention or concentration, and with devekut, a 'cleaving' or continuous attachment to God. These emphases, drawn from the Kabbalah (and especially its Lurianic form) but turned toward an inward, affective, and accessible piety, became the spiritual core of the Hasidic movement that grew from his circle, chiefly through his disciple Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezeritch.

A distinctive difficulty attends any account of the Besht: he wrote almost nothing himself. His teaching survives at second hand, in the sayings and homilies preserved by his disciples and gathered in later Hasidic anthologies such as the Keter Shem Tov and the Tzava'at ha-Rivash (the 'Testament', collecting directives of the Besht and the Maggid), and in the hagiographic Shivchei ha-Besht ('In Praise of the Baal Shem Tov', first printed 1814), a collection of legends about his life and wonders. The principal text plausibly from his own hand is his letter to his brother-in-law Gershon of Kutov, the so-called Iggeret ha-Kodesh or 'Holy Epistle', which recounts an ascent of the soul in which the Besht asks the messiah when he will come and is answered that it will be when the Besht's teaching and 'wellsprings' are spread abroad. Modern scholarship on the Besht (Gershom Scholem, and in detail Moshe Rosman in Founder of Hasidism and Immanuel Etkes in The Besht) has sought the historical figure behind the hagiography, Rosman drawing on Polish and Hebrew archival documents to reconstruct his social and economic context and Etkes situating him among the ba'alei shem and mystics of his day; these reconstructions are recorded here as scholarship alongside the tradition's own reverent account, with the hagiographic and the historical Besht distinguished and neither treated as simply identical to the other.
editorial_notes: This entry presents the Baal Shem Tov on Judaism's own terms as the revered mystic and healer at the origin of Hasidism, and is HONEST about the sourcing problem central to him: he wrote almost nothing, so the load-bearing sources for his teaching are (i) the disciples' anthologies (Tzava'at ha-Rivash and Keter Shem Tov, open on Sefaria), (ii) the hagiographic Shivchei ha-Besht (legend, labelled as such), (iii) his 'Holy Epistle' to Gershon of Kutov (the one text plausibly from his own hand), and (iv) the modern scholarship (Rosman, Etkes, Scholem). His teachings (divine immanence, joyful and wholehearted service, kavvanah, devekut) are ATTRIBUTED to him as transmitted, and DESCRIBED, not adjudicated (neutrality #1, #4). The entry deliberately DISTINGUISHES the hagiographic Besht (Shivchei ha-Besht, the founder-legend) from the historical Besht recoverable by scholarship (Rosman, Etkes), attributing each and treating neither as simply the other (neutrality #1, #5); the very claim that he 'founded' Hasidism is given as the movement's account plus the scholarly debate, not as flat fact. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-#9: (a) academic historians (Scholem, Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism, 'Hasidism: The Latest Phase'; and the archival/critical work of Rosman and Etkes); (b) the tradition's own primary texts, open and auditable on Sefaria (Tzava'at ha-Rivash, read line-by-line and quoted verbatim, and Keter Shem Tov); (c) contemporary Jewish scholars who are also its leading historians (Moshe Rosman; Immanuel Etkes). No encyclopedias (no Encyclopaedia Judaica, no Jewish Encyclopedia, no YIVO Encyclopedia, no Chabad.org, no My Jewish Learning), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. CONFIDENCE / HONESTY: the Tzava'at ha-Rivash on Sefaria is rated HIGH because sections 1, 2, and 44 were read line-by-line and the load-bearing lines on wholehearted/simple service (temimut), serving God 'with joy', and keeping God 'before me always' were confirmed verbatim (English CC-BY, Markel 2020; Hebrew public domain, Warsaw 1913), so his teaching is carried by an open primary and the entry is verifiable / verified-eligible on that basis; Keter Shem Tov is likewise open on Sefaria but its cited opening bears on divine knowing rather than the joy/devekut spine, so it is rated medium here. The secondary works (Scholem, Rosman, Etkes) are medium, verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher and library records, not read line-by-line, and carry the same ratings as in the hasidism entry in this batch. The 'Holy Epistle' is cited via the scholarship that edits and analyzes it (Rosman) rather than a verbatim open text, and its content is stated as attributed. Relation targets: hasidism and kabbalah are same-batch / existing targets, isaac-luria exists; maggid-of-mezeritch, gershon-of-kutov, devekut, tzaddik, and shivchei-ha-besht are dangling forward references to not-yet-created entities.

lifespan.note: c. 1698-1760 CE. Birth is traditionally placed c. 1698-1700 in Okopy (Okup) in Podolia and is approximate; he was active as a ba'al shem and mystic chiefly in Podolia (notably at Medzhybizh / Mezhbizh), where he died in 1760. Dates are approximate; the reconstruction of his biography from documentary rather than hagiographic sources is the subject of modern scholarship (Rosman, Etkes).

relations.note: The Hasidic movement reveres Israel ben Eliezer as its founder, and the standard scholarship traces the movement to his circle; how far he intended to found a movement is debated (Etkes, Rosman) and is noted in the description and editorial notes as attributed, not settled. The hasidism entry is authored in the same batch.

The Besht's teaching draws on the Kabbalah, and especially its Lurianic form (the divine sparks, kavvanah, and mystical intention in prayer), turned toward an inward and affective piety. The kabbalah entry exists.

The Lurianic Kabbalah of Isaac Luria (the Ari), with its sparks, tikkun, and emphasis on mystical intention (kavvanah), is among the principal mystical sources on which the Besht's teaching draws. The isaac-luria entry exists.

Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezeritch (d. 1772), was the Besht's leading disciple and successor, who organized and disseminated Hasidism after him and trained the circle from which the major courts descend. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure entity.

Devekut, continuous 'cleaving' or attachment to God cultivated in and beyond prayer, is central to the Besht's teaching as transmitted (Tzava'at ha-Rivash 2, 'I have set HaShem before me always'); stated as his attributed teaching. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created concept.

The Besht is the paradigm of the tzaddik/rebbe, the charismatic holy leader whose closeness to God the later Hasidic movement made its central institution; stated as attributed. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created concept.

The Shivchei ha-Besht ('In Praise of the Baal Shem Tov', first printed 1814) is the earliest collection of hagiographic legends about him and a principal (hagiographic, not documentary) source for his life; the hagiographic and historical Besht are distinguished in this entry. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created work entity.

Abraham Gershon of Kutov (Kitov) was the Besht's brother-in-law and the recipient of the 'Holy Epistle' (Iggeret ha-Kodesh), the text most plausibly from the Besht's own hand, which recounts his ascent of the soul and dialogue with the messiah. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure entity.

sources.citation: Tzava'at ha-Rivash (Tzava'at HaRivash, the 'Testament of the Rivash', i.e. of Rabbi Israel Baal Shem), open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria; a collection of the directives and teachings of the Baal Shem Tov and his disciple the Maggid of Mezeritch. Cited as the openly-auditable primary text carrying the Besht's transmitted teaching, read line-by-line: section 1, 'Rabbi Yisrael Baal Shem, peace be upon him, taught us to be wholehearted in our service of HaShem, blessed is He, and to serve Him with simplicity' (temimut); section 44, against the melancholy induced by the evil inclination, 'On the contrary, I will serve Him with joy' (simcha over sadness); and section 2, 'I have set HaShem before me always', developed as 'a very lofty level' of continuous attachment to God (devekut). These lines were read and verified verbatim as the load-bearing text for his teaching on joyful, wholehearted, God-attentive service.
sources.license: English: CC-BY (The Way of the Baal Shem Tov, adapted into English by Rabbi Amiram Markel and Yehudah Shimon Markel, 2020, via neirot.com). Hebrew: public domain (Warsaw, 1913). Openly auditable; read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Keter Shem Tov (Keter Shem Tov, the 'Crown of the Good Name'), open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria; the earliest printed anthology (first published 1794-5, Zolkiew edition) of the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov, gathered from the works of his disciples. Cited as a further open primary collection of his transmitted teaching; its opening (Keter Shem Tov 1) preserves a saying 'from the Besht' on two kinds of not-knowing and the limits of the knowledge of God. Read at the passage level; the cited opening bears on divine unknowability rather than the joy/devekut spine, so it is rated medium.
sources.license: English: Sefaria community translation (CC0). Hebrew: public domain (Zolkiew, 1794-5). Openly auditable; read at the passage level.

sources.citation: Rosman, Moshe. Founder of Hasidism: A Quest for the Historical Ba'al Shem Tov. Contraversions 5. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1996 (reprinted, with a new introduction, Oxford: Littman Library, 2013). A contemporary Jewish scholar's archival reconstruction of the Besht from Polish and Hebrew documents, distinguishing the historical figure and his context (in Medzhybizh) from the later hagiographic image, and analyzing the sources including the 'Holy Epistle' to Gershon of Kutov; cited as the load-bearing historical and contemporary-Jewish-scholarship lens on the historical Besht. Verified to exist and be on-topic (University of California Press record; 1996 National Jewish Book Award for Jewish History), not read line-by-line.

Etkes, Immanuel. The Besht: Magician, Mystic, and Leader. Translated by Saadya Sternberg. The Tauber Institute Series for the Study of European Jewry. Waltham: Brandeis University Press, 2005. A contemporary Jewish scholar's study of the Baal Shem Tov as ba'al shem (magician-healer), mystic, and leader, situating him among the kabbalists and healers of his day and examining the hagiographic and documentary evidence; cited as the historical and contemporary-Jewish-scholarship lens on his roles and personality and on the question of his intentions. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Brandeis University Press / University Press of New England record; History of Religions review), not read line-by-line.

Scholem, Gershom. Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism. New York: Schocken Books, 1941 (many later printings). The foundational modern academic study of Jewish mysticism, whose final lecture, 'Hasidism: The Latest Phase', treats the Besht and the movement's mystical teaching (devekut, joy, the neutralization of messianism) and their roots in Kabbalah; cited as the academic-historian lens. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Schocken / Penguin Random House record; Internet Archive), not read line-by-line.
